topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The worn cotton of the blanket felt heavy against my skin, a welcome weight as my eyelids grew impossibly heavy. I was trying to focus on the faint hum of the refrigerator, but the edges of my vision were softening, blurring the familiar shapes of the room. Then, the mail arrived. A thick, glossy brochure, tucked beneath the utility bill. My fingers, sluggish and uncoordinated, fumbled with the envelope. The headline screamed in bold print: "Building Conversion Imminent: Prepare for Condominium Transition." A slow, dull ache settled behind my eyes. I let the brochure drop onto the floor, the sound swallowed instantly by the quiet settling of the late hour. All I could manage was a slow, dragging exhale, sinking deeper into the mattress, the information receding into a distant, unimportant buzz.

[story 2]

I blinked several times, the overhead light seeming too bright, too insistent. My limbs felt weighted, like sinking through deep water. I dragged myself to the window, intending only to look at the streetlights, but the sheer effort of movement was immense. It wasn't just the fatigue; there was a strange, low-frequency anxiety vibrating beneath my ribs. I picked up my phone, intending to check the time, but my thumb hovered uselessly over the screen. That’s when I saw it—a notice taped to the communal bulletin board, stark white against the faded cork. It detailed the impending change of ownership structure. A wave of profound weariness washed over me, making my head tilt forward until my chin rested on my chest. The world outside the window seemed to slow down, each passing car a distant, muted blur.

[story 3]

The silence of the apartment was oppressive, thick enough to lean against. My thoughts were slow-moving molasses, each idea requiring an unreasonable amount of energy to form. I was halfway through a routine evening stretch when I noticed the stack of papers on the kitchen counter. They weren't bills; they were architectural renderings. My gaze drifted over the blueprints, and a cold certainty began to seep into my bones. The realization hit me not with a jolt, but with a gradual, inescapable sinking sensation, like being submerged in cool, dense liquid. I let my shoulders slump entirely, the tension draining out of me until I felt utterly inert. I didn't move to read further; the sheer weight of the impending shift was enough to pull me under, leaving only a profound, heavy stillness.
